findings.description: Header logo and agency header block are repositioned and resized in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows the NC Water Resources logo at upper left and the governor/secretary/director names at upper right in a balanced header layout. The HTML places the logo and agency/address block stacked at the top left, changing the visual placement of the header graphics and text.

findings.description: Memo header formatting is simplified and less formally aligned in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF uses a centered underlined 'MEMORANDUM' title and aligned To/From/Subject fields with tabular spacing. The HTML preserves the text but converts the field layout into more linear left-aligned lines with reduced alignment fidelity.

findings.description: Page-level layout and whitespace grouping are looser in the HTML introduction page.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF has large top whitespace and a formal memorandum layout with centered content block. The HTML compresses the page into a more continuous web-style flow, reducing the original spacing structure though sections remain intact.

findings.description: Section heading styling is lighter in the HTML than in the PDF.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF shows 'I. History/Background' and 'II. March 10, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary' in heavier serif styling. The HTML keeps headings distinct but with a simpler sans-serif/web-style appearance and less visual emphasis.

findings.description: Bullet list typography differs from the PDF, with less formal indentation and emphasis.
findings.evidence: On page 2, the PDF uses compact bullets and serif text with hanging indentation. The HTML uses web bullets and broader spacing; content is preserved, but the visual list formatting is less closely matched.

findings.description: Underlined topic labels in the oral comments summary are converted to bold labels in HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 3, the PDF formats entries such as 'Agriculture', 'Air Quality', and other topic labels as underlined headings within bullets. The HTML renders these labels as bold bullet leads, preserving structure and meaning but altering the original emphasis style.

findings.description: Underlined topic labels in the written comments summary are converted to bold labels in HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 4, the PDF uses underlined lead terms like 'Beneficial Reuse', 'Costs', and 'Dam Safety'. The HTML uses bold bullet labels instead of underlining, retaining readability but not the original formatting.

findings.description: Attachment list formatting is simplified in the HTML.
findings.evidence: On page 5, the PDF presents the attachment list with numbered items and wider indentation in a formal report style. The HTML keeps the numbered list and content, but the spacing and alignment are more compact and web-like.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy does not accurately reflect the document structure because all numbered section headings are encoded as h2, while the PDF presents them as peer top-level sections under the memorandum title. This is a mild structural mismatch rather than a content loss.
findings.evidence: HTML uses one h1 for "MEMORANDUM" and h2 for "I. History/Background", "II. March 10, 2016 Public Meeting and Oral Comments Summary", "III. Written Public Comment Summary", and "IV. Attachments". In the PDF, these numbered sections are the primary body section headings beneath the title, with no deeper subheading hierarchy shown.

findings.description: One sentence is materially altered in the oral comments summary: the PDF says concerns were about letters from DHHS that 'retracted the “do not drink” recommendations,' while the HTML changes this to 'letters from the Department of Health and Human Services that retracted the “do not drink” recommendations.' Expansion of the agency name is acceptable, but the HTML also changes nearby wording and introduces awkward phrasing that departs from the source. Additionally, a page-break split in the written comments section creates a separate bullet for a continuation sentence, altering structure.
findings.evidence:
PDF page 3 under "Private Well Issues": "Some concerns were about the letters from the Department of Health and Human Services that retracted the “do not drink” recommendations." HTML contains similar text, but on page 4-5 split the PDF sentence under "Groundwater Assessments" continues as normal prose: "One comment suggested that capping the ash will not protect against seeps and leaks getting into the groundwater and private wells." In HTML page 5 this continuation is turned into its own list item: "
  • comment suggested that capping the ash will not protect against seeps and leaks getting into the groundwater and private wells.
    • "
